chunk_summary: Maternal nutrition and health significantly influence intergenerational stunting risk, emphasizing dyad-based interventions, adolescent nutrition, and anemia prevention to break the cycle of chronic undernutrition.
learning_objectives: Understand the intergenerational pathways linking maternal undernutrition to stunting in children.
Identify actionable interventions for maternal nutrition during antenatal and postnatal care.
Recognize the importance of adolescent nutrition and anemia prevention in stunting risk reduction.
Learn how maternal mental health impacts responsive feeding and child nutrition outcomes.
key_facts: Maternal anemia is clinically relevant and linked to poor pregnancy outcomes and reduced caregiving capacity postpartum.
Low birth weight (LBW), preterm birth, and poor early weight gain are key indicators of stunting risk.
The first 1000 days are critical for intensified nutrition and infection-prevention support.
Adolescent girls who are stunted are at higher risk of entering pregnancy with poor reserves, perpetuating stunting risk.
Iron–folic acid and calcium supplementation policies are actionable components of stunting prevention in Indian clinical workflows.
reference_quote: Maternal anemia is common and clinically relevant because it is linked to poor pregnancy outcomes and can reduce maternal caregiving capacity postpartum through fatigue and reduced health-seeking. (Source: NFHS-5, Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India)
chunk_heading: STUNTING, CHRONIC UNDERNUTRITION & DETERMINANTS;MATERNAL NUTRITION AND INTERGENERATIONAL RISK FACTORS FOR STUNTING;
